findings.title: Shell command injection via eval of Python output in all agent scripts
findings.description: All 7 agent scripts (qa-scan.sh, seo-audit.sh, ada-audit.sh, compliance-audit.sh, monetization-audit.sh, product-audit.sh, fix-bugs.sh) use 'eval "$(python3 -c ...)"' to parse YAML config. The Python code interpolates shell variables ($REPO_NAME, $TARGET_REPO, $QA_ROOT) directly into the Python string via bash expansion. A specially crafted repo name or target path containing shell metacharacters (e.g., backticks, $(), semicolons) could escape the Python repr() and execute arbitrary shell commands.
findings.evidence:
eval "$(python3 -c "
import yaml, sys
with open('$QA_ROOT/config/targets.yaml') as f:
    cfg = yaml.safe_load(f)
for t in cfg.get('targets', []):
    if t['name'] == '$REPO_NAME' or t.get('path','') == '$TARGET_REPO':
        print(f'LINT_CMD={repr(t.get(\"lint_command\",\"\"))}')
..." 2>/dev/null || true)"
findings.impact: An attacker who can control the TARGET_REPO path or a repo name in targets.yaml could achieve arbitrary command execution. The eval pattern is especially dangerous because it runs the output of one interpreter through the shell's eval, doubling the attack surface. This pattern is repeated in all 7 agent scripts.
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace the eval+python3 pattern with a safer approach: use python3 to write values to a temp file, then source that file, or use python3 -c to print env-safe export statements that are validated before eval. Alternatively, parse YAML with a dedicated script that outputs shell-safe assignments: python3 scripts/parse-config.py --repo "$REPO_NAME" --field lint_command, and capture via command substitution without eval.

findings.title: API server binds to 0.0.0.0 with no authentication by default
findings.description: The api-server.py listens on all interfaces (0.0.0.0) by default at line 380. The api_key config field defaults to empty string, which disables authentication entirely. Combined, this means anyone on the network can trigger scans, which launch arbitrary Claude sessions against configured target repos.
findings.evidence: server = http.server.HTTPServer(("0.0.0.0", port), APIHandler)
findings.impact: Any host on the local network can trigger scans, consuming compute resources (Claude API calls) and potentially modifying codebases if the fix agent is triggered. The scan results could also leak sensitive code information.
findings.fix_suggestion: Bind to 127.0.0.1 by default instead of 0.0.0.0. Add a --bind flag for explicit override. Require api_key to be non-empty when binding to non-loopback addresses. Print a prominent warning if no API key is set.

findings.title: api-config.yaml not in .gitignore, may expose local filesystem paths
findings.description: The file config/api-config.yaml contains local filesystem paths (/home/merm/projects/...) and is NOT listed in .gitignore. Only qa-config.yaml and targets.yaml are gitignored. If committed, this exposes the directory structure of the development machine and potentially API keys if they are ever set.
findings.evidence:
targets:
  thenewbeautifulme: "/home/merm/projects/thenewbeautifulme"
  photo-gallery: "/home/merm/projects/photo-gallery"
  codyjo.com: "/home/merm/projects/codyjo.com"
findings.impact: Exposes local filesystem layout. If an api_key is ever configured, it would be committed to the repository in plaintext.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add 'config/api-config.yaml' to .gitignore. The example file (api-config.example.yaml) already exists and is sufficient for documentation.

findings.title: fix-bugs.sh uses eval on deploy_command from YAML config
findings.description: At line 130, fix-bugs.sh runs: (cd "$TARGET_REPO" && eval "$DEPLOY_CMD"). The DEPLOY_CMD value comes from targets.yaml via the same eval+python3 pattern. While the user controls targets.yaml, this double-eval creates a chain where malicious YAML values could execute arbitrary commands. The deploy_command field is particularly dangerous because it is explicitly designed to run shell commands.
findings.evidence: (cd "$TARGET_REPO" && eval "$DEPLOY_CMD")
findings.impact: If targets.yaml is compromised (e.g., via a supply chain attack or misconfigured permissions), arbitrary commands run with the user's full privileges.
findings.fix_suggestion: Instead of eval, use bash -c "$DEPLOY_CMD" for a single level of shell interpretation, or better yet, validate DEPLOY_CMD against an allowlist of known safe commands. Consider running deploy commands in a restricted shell or container.

findings.title: Dashboard server allows unrestricted CORS (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *)
findings.description: The dashboard-server.py sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin to '*' for all responses (line 104). This allows any website to make API calls to the dashboard server's scan endpoints, potentially triggering scans from a malicious web page if the server is running.
findings.evidence: self.send_header("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*")
findings.impact: Any website a user visits could trigger scan operations on the locally running dashboard server via CORS requests, leading to resource consumption and potential code exposure.
findings.fix_suggestion: Restrict CORS to specific allowed origins. At minimum, only allow localhost and the configured admin subdomains. Apply the same CORS restriction pattern used in api-server.py, which reads allowed_origins from config.

findings.title: Missing CSP headers on all dashboard HTML files
findings.description: None of the 10 dashboard HTML files include a Content-Security-Policy meta tag or header. The dashboards use inline scripts and styles extensively, and load external fonts from Google Fonts. Without CSP, any XSS vulnerability could load arbitrary external scripts.
findings.evidence: No <meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy" ...> found in any dashboard HTML file
findings.impact: Without CSP, if an XSS vulnerability is exploited (e.g., through unsanitized JSON data rendered via innerHTML), an attacker could load external scripts, exfiltrate data, or modify the page in any way. CSP provides defense-in-depth.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a CSP meta tag to each dashboard HTML file. Start with a permissive policy that covers current usage: <meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy" content="default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'self';">. Then tighten incrementally.

findings.title: XSS risk: seo.html, ada.html, monetization.html, product.html lack escapeHtml function
findings.description: The dashboards seo.html, ada.html, monetization.html, and product.html render JSON data from findings files directly into innerHTML template literals without any HTML escaping. While index.html, qa.html, jobs.html, and compliance.html define and use an escapeHtml() function, these 4 dashboards do not. Data fields like 'title', 'description', 'file', and 'evidence' from agent-generated JSON are rendered raw.
findings.evidence: tbody.innerHTML = list.map(f => { ... }).join('');  // No escapeHtml on f.title, f.description, f.file
findings.impact: If a scanned repository contains files or findings with titles containing HTML/JS payloads (e.g., a file named ''), the dashboard would execute that code. The risk is partially mitigated because agents generate the data, but agent output is not fully controlled.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add the escapeHtml() function (already used in index.html) to all dashboard files and apply it to all user/agent-derived text rendered via innerHTML: f.title, f.description, f.file, f.evidence, f.fix_suggestion.

findings.title: All dashboards suppress focus outlines with 'outline: none'
findings.description: 7 out of 10 dashboard HTML files suppress the default focus outline on form elements using 'outline: none' in :focus styles. This removes the visual indicator that keyboard users rely on to know which element is focused, violating WCAG 2.4.7 (Focus Visible).
findings.evidence: .filter-bar select:focus, .filter-bar input:focus { outline: none; border-color: var(--accent); }
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users cannot visually track which element has focus, making the dashboards effectively unusable for people who cannot use a mouse. This is a WCAG 2.1 AA failure.
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace 'outline: none' with a visible focus indicator: use 'outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 2px;' or add a focus-visible polyfill that only suppresses outlines for mouse users: ':focus:not(:focus-visible) { outline: none; } :focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); }'

findings.title: No skip navigation links on any dashboard page
findings.description: None of the 10 dashboard HTML files include a 'Skip to content' link. This is especially problematic because the site-branding.js injects a full navigation header, forcing keyboard users to tab through many links before reaching the main content. Ironically, the ADA dashboard itself identifies missing skip-nav as a finding in audited sites.
findings.evidence: <body><div class="container">  <!-- No skip-to-content link -->
findings.impact: Keyboard users must tab through the entire injected site header navigation (8+ links) and dashboard header before reaching interactive content. This violates WCAG 2.4.1 (Bypass Blocks).
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add a skip-to-content link as the first child of : Skip to content, with CSS: .skip-link { position: absolute; top: -40px; left: 0; z-index: 1000; } .skip-link:focus { top: 0; }. Wrap main content in
.

findings.title: Dashboards lack semantic HTML landmarks (<main>, <nav>, <section>)
findings.description: The dashboard HTML files use <div> elements exclusively for layout. None use <main>, <nav>, <section>, or <article> elements. The only semantic element is <header>, used minimally. Screen readers rely on landmark elements to help users navigate page sections efficiently.
findings.evidence: <div class="container"> ... <div class="stats-grid"> ... <div class="dept-grid"> ... <div class="activity-section">  <!-- All div, no landmarks -->
findings.impact: Screen reader users have no landmark navigation available. They must traverse the entire DOM linearly, making the dashboards very difficult to use. Violates WCAG 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships).
findings.fix_suggestion: Wrap main content in <main role="main">. Use <nav> for navigation sections. Use <section> with aria-label for major content areas (stats, department grid, activity feed). Use <footer> for the footer.

findings.title: Color contrast issues: --text-muted (#5a5a70) on --bg (#0a0a0f) fails WCAG AA
findings.description: The CSS variable --text-muted (#5a5a70) is used extensively for labels, timestamps, and metadata text across all dashboards. Against the dark background (#0a0a0f), this yields a contrast ratio of approximately 3.4:1, which fails the WCAG AA minimum of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text.
findings.evidence: --text-muted: #5a5a70;  /* Used on: stat-label, dept-desc, dept-last-scan, activity-time, section-badge, footer */
findings.impact: Users with low vision or color blindness may be unable to read muted text elements including labels, timestamps, and descriptive metadata. This affects readability for a significant portion of users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Increase the lightness of --text-muted to at least #7a7a94 (approximately 4.5:1 contrast ratio against #0a0a0f) or #8888a0 (already used as --text-dim, which passes). Review all uses of --text-muted and consider whether --text-dim should be used instead.

findings.title: Massive code duplication across dashboard HTML files (CSS and JS)
findings.description: All 10 dashboard HTML files contain near-identical CSS reset blocks (~40 lines), identical CSS variable definitions (~40 lines), identical header/footer markup, and identical utility functions (escapeHtml, timeAgo, formatTime). Each file is a self-contained 800-1900 line monolith. The only shared code is site-branding.js.
findings.evidence: Each file independently defines: :root { --bg: #0a0a0f; --surface: #12121a; --surface-2: #1a1a26; ... } with identical values
findings.impact: A color or font change requires editing 10+ files. Bugs in shared logic (like the missing escapeHtml in 4 dashboards) arise because each file evolves independently. Increases maintenance burden significantly.
findings.fix_suggestion: Extract common CSS into a shared dashboard.css file. Extract common JS utilities (escapeHtml, timeAgo, formatTime, data loading, rendering helpers) into a shared dashboard.js file. This reduces each dashboard file to only its unique content.

findings.title: job-status.sh has excessive code duplication in CLI mode
findings.description: The CLI mode section (lines 255-332) repeats the same 6 variable assignments (QA_ROOT, _JOB_STATUS_DIR, _JOBS_FILE, _JOBS_DASH_FILE, _JOBS_HISTORY_FILE, _JOBS_DASH_HISTORY_FILE, _JOBS_LOCK) identically in each of the 4 case branches (init, start, finish, finalize). This is 24 duplicated lines.
findings.evidence:
case "${1:-}" in
  init)
    QA_ROOT=...
    _JOB_STATUS_DIR=...
    # ... 6 vars repeated in each branch
  start)
    QA_ROOT=...
    # ... same 6 vars again
findings.impact: If a path variable needs updating, it must be changed in 4 places. Easy to introduce bugs by updating one branch but missing another.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move the variable assignments before the case statement so they are set once and shared across all branches.

findings.title: Python scripts use unquoted file paths in f-strings passed to open()
findings.description: In job-status.sh, the inline Python code uses bash variable expansion inside Python strings for file paths: open('$_JOBS_FILE'), open('$QA_ROOT/config/targets.yaml'), etc. If any path contains a single quote, the Python code breaks and could potentially be exploited for injection.
findings.evidence:
python3 -c "
import json, sys, os
...
path = '$_JOBS_FILE'
if os.path.exists(path):
    with open(path) as f:
        data = json.load(f)"
findings.impact: Path traversal or code injection if file paths contain single quotes. While unlikely in normal usage, this violates secure coding practices for a tool that processes arbitrary repository names.
findings.fix_suggestion: Pass file paths as arguments to the Python script instead of interpolating them into the code string. Use sys.argv or environment variables: JOBS_FILE="$_JOBS_FILE" python3 -c "import os; path = os.environ['JOBS_FILE']; ..."

findings.title: watch.sh uses unquoted $SYNC_FLAG variable in command invocation
findings.description: At line 73, watch.sh passes $SYNC_FLAG without quotes: bash "$SCRIPT_DIR/fix-bugs.sh" "$TARGET_REPO" $SYNC_FLAG. While intentional (empty when not set), this relies on word-splitting behavior and is inconsistent with the rest of the codebase which properly quotes all variables.
findings.evidence: bash "$SCRIPT_DIR/fix-bugs.sh" "$TARGET_REPO" $SYNC_FLAG || echo "Fix agent exited with error"
findings.impact: Minor: unquoted variable could cause unexpected behavior if SYNC_FLAG somehow contained spaces or glob characters. The real concern is inconsistency with the otherwise careful quoting discipline across the codebase.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use an array for arguments: local args=("$TARGET_REPO"); [ "$SYNC" = true ] && args+=(--sync); bash "$SCRIPT_DIR/fix-bugs.sh" "${args[@]}"

findings.title: fix-bugs.sh does not track exit code like other agent scripts
findings.description: All other agent scripts (qa-scan.sh, seo-audit.sh, etc.) capture the exit code with '&& _EXIT_CODE=0 || _EXIT_CODE=$?' and call job_finish. fix-bugs.sh at line 119 runs claude --print without capturing the exit code and without calling job_start/job_finish. This means fix operations are not tracked in the job status system.
findings.evidence:
claude --print "$FIX_PROMPT" \
  --allowedTools "Read,Glob,Grep,Bash,Write,Edit,Agent" \
  --add-dir "$TARGET_REPO"
findings.impact: Fix job failures are silently ignored. The jobs dashboard cannot track fix operation status. If the fix agent fails, the script continues to deploy and sync as if it succeeded.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add job_start/job_finish tracking, and capture the exit code: source "$QA_ROOT/scripts/job-status.sh"; job_start "fix"; claude --print ... && _EXIT_CODE=0 || _EXIT_CODE=$?; job_finish "fix" "$_EXIT_CODE"; [ "$_EXIT_CODE" -ne 0 ] && exit "$_EXIT_CODE"

findings.title: Grafana default credentials exposed in Makefile
findings.description: The Makefile at line 160 prints hardcoded default credentials: 'admin / breakpoint' for Grafana. While this is a local development tool, default credentials in source code are a security concern.
findings.evidence: @echo "Grafana running at http://localhost:3333 (admin / breakpoint)"
findings.impact: If the Grafana instance is accidentally exposed to the network, anyone can access it with the documented default credentials.
findings.fix_suggestion: Document the default credentials in a separate file that is gitignored, or prompt the user to set credentials during setup. At minimum, add a comment encouraging the user to change the default password after first login.

findings.title: CONFIG_FILE variable set but never used in 5 agent scripts
findings.description: Five agent scripts (seo-audit.sh, ada-audit.sh, compliance-audit.sh, monetization-audit.sh, product-audit.sh) set CONFIG_FILE="$QA_ROOT/config/qa-config.yaml" at line 17/18 but never reference it. Only qa-scan.sh and fix-bugs.sh actually use config values (lint/test commands).
findings.evidence: CONFIG_FILE="$QA_ROOT/config/qa-config.yaml"  # Never used in this script
findings.impact: Dead code that could mislead developers into thinking the config file is being read. Minor maintenance burden.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove the unused CONFIG_FILE assignment from the 5 scripts that do not use it, or actually read relevant config from it.

findings.title: dashboard-server.py has import inside loop (import time in nested function)
findings.description: In dashboard-server.py, the _run_sequential function at line 196 imports 'time' inside a while loop body. While Python caches imports, placing an import inside a loop is bad practice and makes dependencies non-obvious.
findings.impact: Minor code quality issue. The import is executed on every loop iteration though Python caches it after the first call.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move 'import time' to the top of the file with the other imports.

findings.title: aggregate-results.py does not validate input directory exists
findings.description: The aggregate function at line 177 takes a results_dir parameter and calls os.listdir() on it without checking if the directory exists first. If the directory doesn't exist, it raises an unhandled FileNotFoundError with no helpful message.
findings.evidence: for repo_name in sorted(os.listdir(results_dir)):  # No check if results_dir exists
findings.impact: Unhelpful error message when results directory is missing. The sync-dashboard.sh script calls this and would fail with a Python traceback instead of a clear error.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a check at the start of aggregate(): if not os.path.isdir(results_dir): print(f'Results directory not found: {results_dir}', file=sys.stderr); sys.exit(1)

findings.title: Each dashboard loads full Google Fonts payload independently
findings.description: Each dashboard HTML file independently loads the same Google Fonts CSS (Inter 300-700 + JetBrains Mono 400-500) via a <link> tag. When navigating between dashboards, the browser may re-evaluate the CSS even though fonts are cached. Additionally, site-branding.js loads yet another font (Playfair Display) for site branding.
findings.evidence: <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@300;400;500;600;700&family=JetBrains+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
findings.impact: Each page load makes an external request to fonts.googleapis.com. The display=swap is good, but self-hosting the fonts would eliminate the external dependency and improve first-paint time by ~100ms.
findings.fix_suggestion: Self-host the font files using @font-face declarations in the shared CSS file. Download Inter and JetBrains Mono woff2 files and serve them from the same S3 bucket. This eliminates the external dependency on Google Fonts CDN.

findings.title: Dashboard HTML files are very large single-file applications (20-57KB each)
findings.description: Each dashboard file is a self-contained SPA ranging from ~20KB (faq.html) to 57KB (qa.html). The inline CSS and JS in each file are not minified. With 10 dashboard files, total deployed size is approximately 350KB of unminified HTML/CSS/JS.
findings.evidence: qa.html: ~57KB, jobs.html: ~55KB, compliance.html: ~50KB — each file contains full CSS + full JS inline
findings.impact: Slower initial load times, especially on slower connections. No tree-shaking or dead code elimination. CloudFront does gzip, which helps, but the source files are still unnecessarily large.
findings.fix_suggestion: Extract shared CSS and JS into separate cacheable files. Consider a lightweight build step that minifies the inline CSS and JS. Even just extracting common code would reduce individual page sizes by ~40%.

findings.title: Data tables in dashboards lack proper scope attributes and captions
findings.description: The qa.html and ada.html dashboards render data tables with <th> elements but without scope='col' or scope='row' attributes. Tables also lack <caption> elements to describe their purpose to screen readers.
findings.evidence: tbody.innerHTML = filteredFindings.map(function(f, i) { return '<tr>...'}).join('');  // No scope on th, no caption on table
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot determine the relationship between header cells and data cells in findings tables. Tables are announced as generic data grids without context.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add scope='col' to all <th> elements in thead. Add <caption class='sr-only'>QA Findings Table</caption> to each table. The sr-only class (visually hidden but screen-reader accessible) is a standard pattern.

findings.title: test-scoring.py uses relative file paths via os.chdir instead of absolute paths
findings.description: At line 491, test-scoring.py changes the working directory to the project root using os.chdir(). All subsequent file operations use relative paths like 'results/codyjo.com/findings.json'. This makes the script fragile — it fails if invoked from a different directory without the chdir, and the chdir affects all subsequent code.
findings.evidence:
script_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
os.chdir(os.path.dirname(script_dir))
findings.impact: Minor fragility. If the script is imported as a module or called in a context where chdir has side effects, file operations could fail or operate on wrong files.
findings.fix_suggestion: Instead of os.chdir, compute PROJECT_ROOT once and use os.path.join(PROJECT_ROOT, 'results', repo, fname) for all file access.

findings.title: setup.sh uses pip3 install without --user flag or venv
findings.description: At line 71, setup.sh runs 'pip3 install --quiet pyyaml' which attempts a system-wide install. This may fail without sudo on some systems, or contaminate the system Python environment.
findings.evidence: pip3 install --quiet pyyaml
findings.impact: Setup may fail with permission errors on systems that require sudo for pip. Could conflict with system package manager versions of PyYAML.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use 'pip3 install --user --quiet pyyaml' for user-local install, or create a project venv: python3 -m venv "$QA_ROOT/.venv" && source "$QA_ROOT/.venv/bin/activate" && pip install pyyaml.

findings.title: qa-config.yaml contains real AWS resource identifiers
findings.description: The qa-config.yaml (which is gitignored) contains real S3 bucket names and CloudFront distribution IDs. While gitignored, these are also present in the Terraform code which is committed. The config is not sensitive per se, but exposure could aid in targeted attacks.
findings.evidence:
bucket: "admin-thenewbeautifulme-site"
cloudfront_id: "E372ZR95FXKVT5"
findings.impact: Informational. AWS resource identifiers are not secrets but could be used for reconnaissance in a targeted attack.
findings.fix_suggestion: No action required since the file is gitignored. Ensure Terraform outputs do not log these values in CI/CD pipelines.

findings.title: backoffice.html appears to be a legacy dashboard with limited use
findings.description: The backoffice.html file is described as 'Public bug report form (legacy)' in CLAUDE.md. It contains a large amount of code (~1900 lines) for a bug report form, task tracker, cost tracker, and team management interface. It loads its own data from data.json and appears to overlap with the newer department-specific dashboards.
findings.evidence: <!-- backoffice.html: 1900 lines, described as 'legacy' in CLAUDE.md -->
findings.impact: Dead code that increases maintenance surface. Gets deployed with every sync-dashboard.sh run, consuming bandwidth and potentially confusing users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Consider deprecating and removing backoffice.html if its functionality has been fully replaced by the department-specific dashboards. If it still serves a purpose, update CLAUDE.md to clarify its role.

findings.title: Monitoring docker-compose.yml references hardcoded admin/breakpoint credentials
findings.description: The monitoring setup includes Grafana with default credentials that are printed in the Makefile. This is documented and intentional for local development, but worth noting for awareness.
findings.evidence: @echo "Grafana running at http://localhost:3333 (admin / breakpoint)"
findings.impact: Informational only. Default credentials for local development tools are acceptable if properly documented and not used in production.
findings.fix_suggestion: No immediate action needed. Add a note in the monitoring README to change credentials if deploying beyond localhost.
